nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving warm lowland rivers, swamps, and canals with murky water, dense vegetation, and muddy or sandy substrates; often found among roots and submerged wood

decorPreferences: large smooth stones and flat rocks for spawning sites
driftwood and robust root structures for shelter and territory marking
robust or artificial plants (real plants will be uprooted during spawning)
open sandy substrate areas for digging

diet.notes: Although classified as an omnivore, the Jack Dempsey is primarily carnivorous in practice. It readily accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, large flakes, and frozen or live foods such as earthworms, crickets, bloodworms, brine shrimp, and krill. Feeder fish are sometimes offered but should be used with caution, as overreliance on them risks intestinal parasites and thiamine deficiency. The species will also consume plant matter and algae. Offering a varied diet that combines meaty and vegetable-based foods is generally recommended. Feed once or twice daily in portions the fish can consume within 2–3 minutes.

goodWith: other large robust Central American cichlids (convict cichlid, firemouth cichlid, green terror)
large South American cichlids (Oscar, severum) in spacious tanks
large armored catfish (plecos, hoplo catfish) as dither or bottom-dwellers
large robust barbs or tinfoil barbs in very large tanks
avoidWith: small fish and nano species (will be eaten or terrorized)
shrimp and small invertebrates (will be eaten)
other territorial cichlids of similar size without sufficient space
Electric Blue Jack Dempsey with standard Jack Dempseys (hybridization risk and aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: Patrick Ch. Apfeld; 2867x1899. Sharp full-body adult male showing the diagnostic dense blue-green/turquoise iridescent spotting over a dark base with red-edged dorsal; well-lit, whole fish in frame. Best overall.
Petruss; 2249x1460. Crisp whole-body lateral shot against green water; vivid turquoise flecking and faint vertical bars visible. Different angle/setting for variety.
Juan Carlos Muor; 900x599. Striking intensely-blue individual, whole fish sharp against a dark background; dramatic high-iridescence look. Lower resolution so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: The Jack Dempsey Cichlid is a large, boldly patterned freshwater cichlid from Central America, recognized by its vivid iridescent blue-green spotting and notably aggressive temperament. Named after the heavyweight boxing champion, it is best kept as a bonded pair in a spacious tank alongside other large, robust fish.
careNotes: The Jack Dempsey Cichlid is native to slow-moving, murky lowland rivers and canals from the Yucatán Peninsula in Mexico south through Guatemala, Honduras, and Belize. The species (Rocio octofasciata) was originally described under the name Cichlasoma octofasciatum; in 2007, Schmitter-Soto established the genus Rocio and transferred the species into it based on distinct morphological characters. The older names Cichlasoma octofasciatum and Archocentrus octofasciatus are still encountered in older texts and in the aquarium trade. The common name honours world heavyweight boxing champion Jack Dempsey (1895–1983), a nod to the species' well-documented aggressive disposition. Adults typically reach 20–25 cm and are sexually dimorphic: males tend to be larger and more intensely coloured, displaying a wider array of iridescent blue-green to turquoise spots and lines; females are generally smaller with a more brownish base coloration and less vivid iridescence. A minimum of 250 L (approximately 65 US gal) is broadly recommended for a pair, with more spacious setups strongly advised when housing alongside other cichlids. The substrate should include areas of at least 5 cm depth in sand or fine gravel to accommodate the species' persistent digging behaviour; flat smooth rocks or slate serve as favoured spawning sites. Live plants are likely to be uprooted during breeding activity or territorial disputes, so robust or artificial alternatives are generally preferable. Regular partial water changes of around 25–30% weekly are advisable, as the Jack Dempsey tends to be sensitive to nitrate accumulation above roughly 40 ppm. While the full tolerable temperature range is 22–30°C, 24–28°C is generally considered optimal; a pH of 6.5–8.0 is broadly accepted, and the species appears to do well in moderately hard to hard water. The Electric Blue Jack Dempsey is a naturally occurring colour morph of the same species, distinguished by reduced melanin and intensified blue iridescence. It tends to show lower overall vigour than the standard form, and mixing the two should be avoided due to both hybridisation risk and aggression. The Jack Dempsey is best kept as a proven bonded pair and will reorganise tank decor extensively.
breedingNotes: The Jack Dempsey is an open substrate-spawner with the strong pair bonding typical of substrate-brooding cichlids. In mature adults, sexing is generally reliable: males tend to be larger and more brilliantly coloured, while females typically have a shorter dorsal fin and reduced iridescence. Conditioning the pair on a varied diet of high-quality live and frozen foods promotes spawning readiness. The pair will select and vigorously clean a flat smooth rock, slate slab, or cleared area of substrate as the spawning site. Females deposit 500–800 adhesive eggs in neat rows, and both parents share guarding duties through the egg and larval stages. Eggs typically hatch in approximately 72 hours at 26°C, with fry becoming free-swimming within 5–7 days. Parental care continues for several weeks, and aggression toward other tankmates becomes extreme during this period — removing all other inhabitants before spawning begins is strongly advised. Fry will accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and microworms from their first feeding. Getting a pair to spawn is considered moderately challenging, primarily because it requires a compatible bonded pair, a suitable flat spawning site, and enough tank volume to manage the pair's elevated aggression during breeding.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rocio octofasciata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Schmitter-Soto 2007 reclassification from Cichlasoma to Rocio), native range (Atlantic slope from Yucatán Peninsula Mexico to Honduras), adult size (males to 25 cm, females smaller), temperature 22–30°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 9–20 dGH, biotope (murky slow-moving lowland rivers/canals), breeding behaviour (substrate-spawner, biparental), aggression, and tankmate guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Rocio octofasciata
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max reported 25 cm TL males), temperature range (22–30°C), native range countries (Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Belize), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet classification (omnivore with strong carnivore tendency), and depth range. Confirmed synonym Cichlasoma octofasciatum and reclassification date. Also verified 'Eight-Banded Cichlid' as accepted common name in FishBase records.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Jack Dempsey Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed minimum tank size (55 gal / ~208 L for single, larger for pairs; 250 L chosen as conservative pairing minimum), diet recommendations (cichlid pellets, earthworms, meaty frozen foods), aggression level, tankmate guidance, and Electric Blue Jack Dempsey colour morph notes (same species, not separate).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Jack Dempsey profile
additional.notes: Supplementary UK trade source. Confirmed reclassification into Rocio, adult size range 20–25 cm, water parameters, aggressive substrate-spawner behaviour, pair bonding, and lifespan up to 10–15 years in captivity. Notes strong pair bond and biparental fry guarding. Confirmed the Electric Blue Jack Dempsey as a colour morph, not a distinct species.

additional.name: Schmitter-Soto, J.J. (2007) — A Systematic Revision of the Genus Archocentrus, with Description of Two New Genera and Six New Species (Teleostei: Cichlidae)
additional.notes: Original taxonomic authority for Rocio octofasciata reclassification. Schmitter-Soto (2007) established the genus Rocio and placed octofasciata within it, separating it from Cichlasoma and Archocentrus based on morphological characters. Previously known as Cichlasoma octofasciatum (Regan, 1903) and Archocentrus octofasciatus in interim literature. The epithet refers to 'eight bands' (octo=eight, fasciata=banded), origin of the alternate common name 'Eight-Banded Cichlid'.

fish.breedingNotes: Open substrate-spawner with strong biparental care. Conditioning the pair on a varied diet of high-quality live and frozen foods promotes spawning. The female lays 500–800 adhesive eggs on a cleaned flat rock or slate slab; eggs typically hatch in around 72 hours at 26°C, with fry becoming free-swimming by day 5–7. Both parents guard the eggs and fry aggressively for several weeks. All other tankmates should be removed during breeding. Fry accept brine shrimp nauplii and microworms from first feeding.
